### Feature generator

> Feature generator that uses a sliding window of n previous timesteps
> and polynomial features to predict the next value, enabling learning
> of temporal patterns and non-linear relationships

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L20"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### FeatureGenerator

>  FeatureGenerator (context_window:int=10, target_window:int=10,
>                        degree:int=1)

*Transforms time series data into feature matrices suitable for machine
learning models. Creates lagged features using a sliding window and
optionally generates polynomial features to capture non-linear
relationships between variables.*

### Benchmark model

We chose a linear regression model as our benchmark because: 1. When
combined with our feature generator, it provides a simple yet effective
baseline 2. The polynomial features allow it to capture non-linear
relationships in the data 3. The sliding window enables learning of
temporal patterns across multiple timesteps 4. Linear regression models
are highly interpretable and computationally efficient

href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L65"

### Model training and comparison

## Scoring

Since we have generated multiple predictions with different parameters,
we will select only the best performing models according to each metric

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L78"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

### BenchmarkScores

>  BenchmarkScores ()

\*A class to compute and compare different error metrics between
predictions and observations.

This class provides a flexible way to compute multiple error metrics
between predicted and observed values. New metrics can be easily added
by implementing them as methods. The new metrics can then be used by
passing their method names as strings to compute_scores().

Example:

\`\`\`python class CustomBenchmarkScores(BenchmarkScores): def mse(self,
pred, obs): error = ((pred - obs)\*\*2).mean(dim=“time”) error.name =
“mse” return error

# Can then be used as:

scores = compute_scores(pred, obs, metrics=\[“mae”, “rmse”, “mse”\])
\`\`\`\*

Based on the scatter plot comparing MAE vs MSE metrics, we can conclude
that polynomial regression with degree 2 and window sizes between 30-50
days provides the optimal predictions. This is evident from the cluster
of points in the lower left corner of the plot, which indicates lower
error rates for both metrics. Specifically, the combinations of degree=2
with windows around 40 days achieve the best balance between Mean
Absolute Error and Mean Squared Error, suggesting these parameters offer
the most accurate and stable predictions without overfitting the data.

## Save prediction data

We save the best model for later use

## Time series verification

Now that we have identified the optimal model parameters, let’s verify
its performance by comparing the predicted discharge values with both
observed values and MGB model predictions. This comparison will be done
across the full 10-day prediction horizon to assess how well our model
maintains its predictive power over time. We’ll visualize these
comparisons using time series plots that show the observed discharge,
our model’s predictions, and the MGB model predictions side by side.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

<a
href="https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal/blob/main/ombs_senegal/benchmark_model.py#L290"
target="_blank" style="float:right; font-size:smaller">source</a>

>                                  mgb:xarray.core.dataarray.DataArray)

for a 10-day horizon.*

We first need to reescale the MGB data
